description: Everything implies itself — even when we say it about everything.

guides.text: We're entering first-order logic. $\forall$ is the **[universal-quantifier|universal quantifier]** — it says 'for all.' The goal $\forall x.\; P(x) \to P(x)$ reads: for every $x$, $P(x)$ [implication|implies] itself. This is the identity theorem from problem 01, generalised to all possible things.

To prove a universal statement, we pick an arbitrary [witness|witness] — a fresh term variable — prove the statement for that witness without assuming anything special about it, then generalise. If it works for a nobody, it works for everybody.
The rule `fix` introduces a fresh term variable, like `assume` does for [proposition|propositions]. It gives us $\{x\} \vdash x$ — an arbitrary $x$ to work with.
Now as if we're still in propositional logic: assume $P(x)$ and prove $P(x)$. The structure is identical to problem 01.
These two live in separate contexts: $\{x\}$ and $\{P(x)\}$. Merge them with `and-intro` so we can use both.
Extract $P(x)$ from the merged context — this is our $P(x) \to P(x)$ conclusion, still under the [witness|witness] $x$.
The finale: `forall-intro` checks that $x$ is fresh (appears nowhere else in the context) and wraps the conclusion: $\forall x.\; P(x) \to P(x)$. The proof stands for all $x$.
